items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
I’m tormented by vague doubts again…
Shpak has a tape recorder,
the ambassador has a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Core phrase is rendered well and keeps the mock-heroic tone (“tormented by vague doubts”). However, the second sentence is an accuracy problem per the reference note: «У Шпака — …, у посла — …» here points to each having LOST/being associated with a missing item (parallel evidence), not current possession. “Has” misstates the situation and weakens the detective-comic logic. Also, the reference stresses not translating as “has.”

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Very pleased, Tsar!
Very pleased—hello, Tsar!

Tsar, very pleased!
Hello, Tsar—very pleased!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The translation preserves the key comic mechanism: repetitive, banal politeness and the absurd insistence on “Tsar” as a social-introduction label. It’s not identical to the reference (it omits the explicit self-identification “I’m the Tsar”), but it still conveys the same era-clash and drunken loopiness. Slight loss of the “introducing oneself” feel, but the humor largely survives.

items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Effectively identical in meaning, register, and punch. Keeps the authoritarian, suddenly-royal persona. No meaningful loss.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Identical to the reference translation; preserves the archaic/high-register melancholy and the comic mismatch with the modern setting.

items.translation:
Walled up!
Walled up, you demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Meaning is mostly conveyed (being sealed in; blaming demons), but the English shifts agency: “you demons” makes demons the addressee/agent, whereas the reference note highlights demons as the subject blamed in a more exclamatory way (“the demons!”). Also “Walled up!” is a bit abrupt/elliptical compared to the plaintive “They’ve walled me in…”, slightly reducing the melodramatic panic tone.

items.translation: That’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Accurate and idiomatic. Slightly less elevated/archaic than “Behold the power…”, but still keeps the religious triumphalism and comic attribution of a mechanical event to faith.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Conveys the command to dance, but loses the specific Soviet/MC-style catchphrase feel (a set, showman-like formula) that the reference highlights. “Everybody dance!” is understandable yet flatter and less characteristically ‘announcer’/party-leader; it also sounds slightly ungrammatical without “Everybody, dance!”

items.translation: Well, this is a lucky visit!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Captures the upbeat self-congratulation at stumbling into a profitable situation. “Lucky visit” is slightly less colloquial/criminally breezy than the reference’s “good call,” but the pragmatic function (cheerful satisfaction) is preserved.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar...
and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Matches the reference’s key creative decision: avoid calling eggplant spread “caviar” in English, preserving the Soviet-food-shortage joke and the inversion of luxury. Rhythm and reveal (“and yes!” … “eggplant!”) are intact.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
Everything, everything I earned
by backbreaking labor,
all is lost: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestic cigarette cases,
three suede jackets.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: The list structure and escalating-count humor are damaged. The source has one suede jacket, then the punch “— три” (three) applied to the jacket at the end; the translation turns it into “three suede jackets,” removing the comic hesitation and the final ‘increment’ beat. It also omits the stammering/ellipsis effect (“куртка… замшевая… Три”) that signals Shpak’s self-correction/exaggeration. This is a major accuracy/creative loss because the joke is specifically about the counting and the delayed “three.”

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you!
And they’ll cure you too!

And they’ll cure me!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Essentially identical in meaning and comic resignation. Minor punctuation/ellipsis differences don’t matter; the rhythmic, calm fatalism is preserved.

summary.interpretation: 2.00 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.40 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
